comment_text.sn19.11:1.4: DN 23:9.1 tells how a man who has escaped a sewer and been provided with all sensual pleasures would not return. Thus the sewer is the inverse of the pleasures of the flesh.

root_text.sn19.11:1.3: “Idhāhaṁ, āvuso, gijjhakūṭā pabbatā orohanto addasaṁ purisaṁ gūthakūpe sasīsakaṁ nimuggaṁ …pe…
root_text.sn19.11:1.4: eso, bhikkhave, satto imasmiṁyeva rājagahe pāradāriko ahosi …pe….

translation_text.sn19.11:1.2: At one time near Rājagaha in the Bamboo Grove …
translation_text.sn19.11:1.3: “Just now, reverend, as I was descending from Vulture’s Peak Mountain I saw a man sunk over his head in a sewer. …” …
translation_text.sn19.11:1.4: “That being used to be an adulterer right here in Rājagaha. …”

variant_text.sn19.11:0.3: Sasīsakasutta → gūthakūpasuttaṁ (bj); kupe nimuggo paradāriko (pts1ed, pts2ed)
